notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Looking at your transaction history, you're running a lean, well-organized setup as a self-employed UX consultant with five accounts: the Chase Ink Business Preferred 4028 handling most business charges, the American Express Blue Business Plus 7721 picking up dining and groceries, the Bluevine Business Checking 8810 receiving client payments, the Capital One 360 Checking 4470 as your personal hub, and the Ally Tax Reserve Savings 5391 holding estimated tax funds. Here's how to route each spending category to maximize rewards given what you're already carrying.
Software subscriptions
Your Chase Ink Business Preferred is already capturing your software spend — AWS, Webflow, Adobe Creative Cloud, Google Workspace, and Figma all flowed through it last month. That's the right call. The Ink Preferred earns 3x Ultimate Rewards points on internet, cable, phone, and many software/online service charges (up to $150,000/year combined across its bonus categories). Keep routing all SaaS and cloud tools through this card first, then check your statement to confirm each vendor triggers the 3x category — some niche providers code as "professional services" instead and would only earn 1x, in which case your Amex Blue Business Plus (2x on everything, up to $50,000/year) is a solid fallback.
Business travel
The Ink Preferred is also the right card here, and your Atlanta trip — Delta Air Lines and the Marriott Marquis — correctly flowed through it. It earns 3x on travel broadly (flights, hotels, car rentals) within the same $150,000 annual cap. For Uber rides categorized as business travel, the Ink Preferred should capture those too, though some Uber charges can code inconsistently — worth monitoring.
Groceries
Your Costco and H-E-B purchases are running through the Amex Blue Business Plus, which earns a flat 2x Membership Rewards on everything. That's reasonable, but groceries are one category where you could potentially do better. A dedicated personal card with 4x at U.S. supermarkets (like the Amex Gold) could double your return here. H-E-B qualifies as a supermarket; Costco typically doesn't, as it codes as a warehouse club. For now, the Blue Business Plus is a fine default — just know there's upside if you ever add a personal grocery card.
Dining
Torchys Tacos and Uchi Austin are currently on your Amex Blue Business Plus (2x). This is your biggest optimization opportunity. A card earning 3x–4x on dining — like the Amex Gold (4x worldwide) or Chase Sapphire Preferred (3x) — could meaningfully increase your return on restaurant spend. Since you dine out regularly for both personal and client meals, this gap compounds over time.
Office supplies
Your Office Depot purchase went through the Chase Ink Business Preferred. While that earns 1x on office supplies (office supply stores aren't in its bonus categories), you're leaving points on the table. The Chase Ink Business Cash earns 5% at office supply stores up to $25,000/year — the single highest reward rate available in this category. If your office supply spend grows, adding the Ink Business Cash would be a meaningful upgrade, and it has no annual fee.
Quick routing summary
Software & SaaS: Chase Ink Business Preferred (3x, verify MCC)
Business travel: Chase Ink Business Preferred (3x)
Groceries (H-E-B): Amex Blue Business Plus (2x, or consider a 4x grocery card)
Dining: Amex Blue Business Plus today (2x), but a 3–4x dining card would be a clear upgrade
Office supplies: Consider Chase Ink Business Cash for 5x — currently going to Ink Preferred at 1x
The most actionable near-term move would be evaluating a dedicated dining card, since that's where your current setup leaves the most rewards on the table given your spending patterns. Would you like to explore which specific card additions make the most sense given your self-employed cashflow and tax-bucket preferences?
